content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660           322-3901           322-6441           322-6444

July 30, 1980


Stanwood C. Johnson
Attorney at Law
3430 Camino Del Rio North, Ste. 300
San Diego, CA 92108
                                          A-80-07-644

                                    Re: Opinion Request
                                        No. 80-005

Dear Mr. Johnson:

     Thank you for your letter of July 1, 1980, requesting
an opinion regarding the disclosure provisions of the Political
Reform Act. Because your question does not raise a signifi-
cant question of interpretation regarding the Political
Reform Act no formal opinion will be issued in response to
it. However, in response to your letter I can advise you
that the Committee for Community Responsibility has to file
a campaign disclosure statement under the Political Reform
Act only if it is a "committee" within the meaning of Govern-
ment Code Section 82013. A committee is defined under Section
82013:

     "Committee" means any person or combination of
persons who directly or indirectly receives contribu-
tions or makes expenditures or contributions for the
purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the
action of the voters for or against the nomination or
election of one or more candidates, or the passage or
defeat of any measure, including any committee or sub-
committee of a political party, whether national, state
or local if:

     (a) Contributions received total five hundred
dollars ($500) or more in a calendar year;

     (b) Independent expenditures total five hundred
dollars ($500) or more in a calendar year; or

     (c) Contributions made to or at the behest of
candidates and committees total five thousand dollars
($5,000) or more in a calendar year.

                                                      82213

Stanwood C. Johnson
July 30, 1980
Page Two


As long as the sole activity of the Committee for Community
Responsibility is the preparation and distribution of candidate
information sheets similar to the one you submitted with
your letter, which merely present the views of candidates on
selected issues, it is not a committee under Government Code
Section 82013, and it need not file any campaign disclosure
statements. This is because such a publication, which is
not made by or at the behest of any candidate and which does
not "expressly advocate" the election or defeat of any candidate, is not considered to have been made for the purpose of
influencing voters under Section 82013. See Buckley v.
Valeo, 424 U.S. 1 (1976). In determining whether future
publications bring your group under Section 82013 you should
consult Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(b)(2)
which establishes standards for determining what constitutes
express advocacy.

I trust this answers your questions. If you wish to
appeal the denial of your opinion request, you may do so
pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321. If you have any
questions, please contact Barbara Milman, an attorney in the
Commission's Legal Division.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim
Executive Director

TP:plh
cc: James Hamilton

.                                .

Stanwood C. Johnson
ATTORNEY AT LAW

Request No. 80 005

3430 CAMINO DEL RIO NORTH, SUITE 300
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92108

(714) 563-8992

Date Received 7-3-80
Response Due 7/17-80

July 1, 1980

Proposed response to
Commirs. due: 7/11-80

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Gentlemen:

    This request for an advisory opinion is made pursuant
to Section 83114(a) of the Government Code. The purpose of
this request is to clarify an ambiguity in the campaign
disclosure laws.

    I have enclosed a copy of the Candidate Information
Sheet that a group of us prepared for the June 3 primary
election. This sheet was distributed in various evangelical
churches in the San Diego area.

    Since we collected and spent far less than $500.00 in the
preparation and distribution of this sheet, it was not necessary
for us to file a campaign disclosure statement. However, we
are uncertain whether such a statement will be necessary in the
future.

    I personally do not believe that the assembling and
disseminating of candidates' positions as we have done falls
under the rubric of influencing for or against the election
of candidates. However, there remains some doubt in our
minds as to this belief due to the lack of clarity in the
statute.

    Therefore, I would like to present the following question
for your opinion. If the Committee for Community Responsibility
should prepare a similar Candidate Information Sheet in the
November election and its cumulative expenditures exceed
$500.00, would it be necessary for this Committee to file a
campaign disclosure statement?

Sincerely, yours,

STANWOOD C. JOHNSON

Enclosure
c: James Hamilton, Deputy District Attorney
SCJ:ntn

CANDIDATE INFORMATION SHEET

ELECTION DAY - TUESDAY, JUNE 3

The following is a digest of the candidates' stands on issues of public morality.
These responses came as a result of a questionnaire sent by the Committee for
Community Responsibility.

41st CONGRESSIONAL DISTRICT: On the Republican ticket, Councilman Bill Lowery
has fought for the ordinance that restricted adult entertainment and massage
parlors. He believes that America will be great as long as it is good, and that
we "cannot cohesively function as a nation without a cornerstone of community values". He strongly
opposes abortion and favors stronger penalties for the use and sale of hard drugs.
Quincy John Workman believes that a representative should vote the way the people
wish on moral issues. Dan McPherson has served on the Board of Directors of Youth
for Christ and was general chairman of the Billy Graham Crusade. He calls himself a "John 3 and John 14 Christian". He opposes abortion and favors stronger
penalties for the use and sale of hard drugs. On the Democratic ticket Arthur
Sanders answered most of the questions on morality by saying it "depends on the
situation". He does however, favor stronger penalties for use and sale of hard
drugs and is opposed to publicly funded abortions. Senator Bob Wilson stated
that his voting record is 100% Pro-Life and anti-abortion. He introduced legislation to combat child pornography and voted against a "gay rights" bill as he
thought it was too broad. He is against legalizing marijuana and favors stronger
penalties for the use and sale of hard drugs. Supervisor Jim Bates likes to attend
church services to be introduced. However, he refused to answer the questions on
public morality. Newspaper accounts have documented his support for a county "gay
rights" ordinance.

42nd CONGRESSIONAL: Neither Republican candidate saw fit to answer our questionnaire. In the Democratic race, incumbent Lionel Van Deerlin would vote to legalize
prostitution, favors publicly funded abortions, and believes that a woman has the
right to terminate her pregnancy for any reason. He also favors a bill to forbid
discrimination on the basis of homosexuality. Samuel Pagwell and Lois Bodie are
both against legalizing prostitution and oppose publicly funded abortions. Neither
favor "gay" rights, and both feel that parental consent should be required before
a minor can be given contraceptives. Bagwell states that he tried to promote
community decency by setting an example to his family, his neighbors, and his
friends. Bodie states she is born of the Spirit and that Biblical principles
must be central and basic in the governance of our society.

39th STATE SENATE DISTRICT: On the Republican ballot, one candidate, Mike Schaefer
said that all three candidates are "good Christian gentlemen". Schaefer is active
in "Pro-Life" in opposing abortions, and he believes that prostitution should remain illegal. Jim Ellis has publically taken many strong moral stands during his
two terms in the California Assembly. He professes Jesus Christ as his Lord and
Savior. He opposes abortions and favors stronger penalties for the use and sale
of hard drugs. Fred Schnaubelt believes that the responsibility for morality lies
with the family, the Church, and the private agencies in that order. He believes
that since government has failed to prevent the use of marijuana and hard drugs,
they should be legalized. Schnaubelt is opposed to public funding of abortions
and believes the solutions for drug abuse and prostitution lies with the Church
and not the government. In the Democratic race, neither candidate would let us
know whether he had any concern for public morality.

- 1 -

77th ASSEMBLY: the Democrat ballot, Floyd McGhee is a member of Atonement
Lutheran Church and has the strong support of his pastor. He opposes publicly
funded abortions and believes that parental consent should be required before a
minor can be given contraceptives. He favors legalizing prostitution and believes
that growing of marijuana for personal use should be legalized. He also favors
equal rights for homosexuals. La Mesa Councilmen Rick Augustine and Fred Nagel
both favor stricter penalties for the use and sale of hard drugs. They oppose
legalizing prostitution and state that the cultivation of marijuana for personal
use should remain against the law. Both are against "gay rights". Augustine
attends Faith Chapel and hopes that "someday all politicians will follow the
Bible". Nagel attends the Evangelical Bible Church of La Mesa and believes that
Biblical principles should be uppermost in the minds of elected officials. Ted
Schramm has worked as a jail chaplain and in many drug treatment programs. He
served as the Associate Director of the White House Office of Drug Abuse Policy.
He strongly opposes any legalization of marijuana possession or cultivation for
personal use, and also favors stronger penalties for the use and sale of hard
drugs. He believes that a woman is entitled to terminate pregnancy in the
first trimester for any reason and that if the wealthy can have abortions, the
government should provide them for the poor. No other Democrats responded to
the questionnaire. On the Republican ballot, Councilman Larry Stirling has organized a monthly Bible study at City Hall. He believes that prostitution should
remain illegal and opposes public funding of abortions. He is against the legalization of marijuana for personal use. Norma Scheuman believes that marijuana
laws should either be changed or enforced. She also opposes legalization of
prostitution and publically funded abortions.

78th ASSEMBLY DISTRICT: Incumbent Assemblyman Larry Kapiloff is unopposed in
the Democratic primary. In the Republican primary Ben Elliot and Ross Tharp gave
identical answers to the questions on morality. Both are strong advocates of a
higher standard of decency in the world. Elliot, who is a member of College
Avenue Baptist Church, has given his campaign over to God. Judge Tharp believes
he has promoted community morality with a "strict no-nonsense" approach that he
claims has sent "more criminals to state prison than any other judge in the county".
Nick Mioslavich believes in publicly funded abortions but also believes that
parental consent should be required before a child can be given contraceptives.
He favors stronger penalties for the sale, but not the use of hard drugs.

SUPERVISOR 2nd DISTRICT: In this nonpartisan race, incumbent Lucille Moore did
not wish to respond to our questionnaire on public morality, nor did Gail Noble.
The newspapers have reported that Supervisor Moore sponsored a "gay rights" ordinance for the county but withdrew it after the public outcry. Jim Graves signed
and returned his questionnaire on moral issues, but he left it blank. Frank Van
Gooslee believes that his fighting two wars to "preserve democracy" shows what
he has done to promote a higher standard of decency and morality in the community.
He opposes abortion and favors the death penalty for those who sell hard drugs.
Paul Fordem, as La Mesa's Mayor, has fought against the proliferation of "X"-
rated entertainment. He opposes "gay rights", opposes publicly funded abortions,
and favors stronger penalties for the use and sale of hard drugs. Curtis Maynard
has worked to place "good reading material in stores". He opposes "gay rights",
publicly funded abortions, and favors stronger penalties for the sale and use of
hard drugs.

Committee for Community Responsibility
Starwood Johnson, Rev. Earl King, Officers
3430 Camino del Rio North, Suite 300
San Diego, California 92108

The Committee for Community Responsibility is comprised of Evangelical Christians
who wish to present information to the voters on issues of public morality. The
Committee works with, but is not a part of, the San Diego Evangelical Association.
No contributions have been accepted from any candidate, party, or campaign committee. Interested persons may review the questionnaires by calling 563-8992 for
an appointment.

Stanwood C. Johnson
ATTORNEY AT LAW

3430 CAMINO DEL RIO NORTH, SUITE 300
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92108

(714) 563-8992


July 30, 1980


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95814

Gentlemen:

     On July 1, 1980, I mailed you a request for an advisory
opinion pursuant to Section 83114(a) of the Government Code.
Enclosed is a copy of that letter.

     It appears that the statutory response time of 14 days
has not been met by your office.  Please respond to my
request as soon as possible.

Sincerely yours,


STANWOOD C. JOHNSON


Enclosure

c:  James Hamilton, Deputy District Attorney

SCJ:ntn

Stanwood C. Johnson
ATTORNEY AT LAW

3430 CAMINO DEL RIO NORTH, SUITE 300
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92108

(714) 563-8992


July 1, 1980


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 307
Sacramento, California 95814

Gentlemen:

This request for an advisory opinion is made pursuant
to Section 83114(a) of the Government Code. The purpose of
this request is to clarify an ambiguity in the campaign
disclosure laws.

I have enclosed a copy of the Candidate Information
Sheet that a group of us prepared for the June 3 primary
election. This sheet was distributed in various evangelical
churches in the San Diego area.

Since we collected and spent far less than $500.00 in the
preparation and distribution of this sheet, it was not necessary
for us to file a campaign disclosure statement. However, we
are uncertain whether such a statement will be necessary in the
future.

I personally do not believe that the assembling and
disseminating of candidates' positions as we have done falls
under the rubric of influencing for or against the election
of candidates. However, there remains some doubt in our
minds as to this belief due to the lack of clarity in the
statute.

Therefore, I would like to present the following question
for your opinion. If the Committee for Community Responsibility
should prepare a similar Candidate Information Sheet in the
November election and its cumulative expenditures exceed
$500.00, would it be necessary for this Committee to file a
campaign disclosure statement?

Sincerely yours,


STANWOOD C. JOHNSON

Enclosure
c: James Hamilton, Deputy District Attorney
SCJBURN
